epigraph.text:
Now we see only a reflection as in a mirror; then we 
shall see face to face.

content.text:
When the discriminant of a quadratic equation is negative, we need 
numbers beyond the real number system. Complex numbers extend our 
number system to include square roots of negative numbers, completing 
the solution set for all quadratic equations.
The Imaginary Unit

For centuries, mathematicians rejected the idea of $\sqrt{-1}$ as 
nonsensical—no real number squared gives a negative result. In the 
16th century, Italian mathematicians discovered that allowing such 
"imaginary" numbers led to real solutions of cubic equations.

content.title: The Imaginary Unit
content.content:
The **imaginary unit** $i$ is defined by
\[
 i = \sqrt{-1} \quad \text{so that} \quad i^2 = -1
\]

content.text:
This definition allows us to express the square root of any negative 
number:

content.title: Square Roots of Negative Numbers
content.content:
For any positive real number $a$:
\[
 \sqrt{-a} = i\sqrt{a}
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Order Matters
content.content:
When simplifying expressions like $\sqrt{-a}$, always write the $i$ 
*before* any radical: write $i\sqrt{7}$, not $\sqrt{7}i$. This 
prevents confusion about whether the $i$ is under the radical.

content.text: Powers of $i$
The powers of $i$ follow a cyclic pattern:
The pattern $i, -1, -i, 1$ repeats every four powers.

content.title: Evaluating powers of $i$
content.solution:
Divide the exponent by 4 and use the remainder:

**(a)** $17 = 4 \times 4 + 1$, so $i^{17} = i^1 = i$

**(b)** $42 = 4 \times 10 + 2$, so $i^{42} = i^2 = -1$

**(c)** $103 = 4 \times 25 + 3$, so $i^{103} = i^3 = -i$

content.text: Complex Numbers

content.title: Complex Number
content.content:
A **complex number** is a number of the form
\[
 a + bi
\]
where $a$ and $b$ are real numbers. The number $a$ is called the 
**real part**, written $\Re(a + bi) = a$, and $b$ 
is called the **imaginary part**, written 
$\Im(a + bi) = b$.

content.text: The set of all complex numbers is denoted $\C$.
**Special cases:**

items: If $b = 0$, then $a + bi = a$ is a real number

If $a = 0$ and $b \neq 0$, then $a + bi = bi$ is a 
 **pure imaginary number**
If $b \neq 0$, then $a + bi$ is a **non-real complex number**

content.text: Operations with Complex Numbers

**Addition and Subtraction:** Combine like terms (real with real, 
imaginary with imaginary).

**Multiplication:** Use the distributive property (FOIL) and 
replace $i^2$ with $-1$.

content.title: Product of conjugates
content.solution:
$$
 (5 + 2i)(5 - 2i) &= 25 - 10i + 10i - 4i^2 \\
 &= 25 - 4(-1) = 25 + 4 = 29
$$

Notice: the product is a real number! This is always true for conjugates.

content.text: Complex Conjugates

content.title: Complex Conjugate
content.content:
The **complex conjugate** of $z = a + bi$ 
is
\[
 \bar{z} = a - bi
\]
The conjugate changes only the sign of the imaginary part.

content.title: Product of Conjugates
content.content:
For any complex number $z = a + bi$:
\[
 z \cdot \bar{z} = (a + bi)(a - bi) = a^2 + b^2
\]
The product of a complex number and its conjugate is always a 
non-negative real number.
content.label: Theorem

content.text: This property is essential for division.
Division of Complex Numbers

To divide complex numbers, multiply numerator and denominator by the 
conjugate of the denominator.
Solving Equations with Complex Solutions

content.title: Quadratic with complex solutions
content.solution:
Using the quadratic formula:
\[
 x = \frac{-4 \pm \sqrt{16 - 52}}{2} = \frac{-4 \pm \sqrt{-36}}{2} = \frac{-4 \pm 6i}{2} = -2 \pm 3i
\]

The solutions are $x = -2 + 3i$ and $x = -2 - 3i$.

Notice: The solutions are complex conjugates of each other!

content.title: Complex Conjugate Root Theorem
content.content:
If a polynomial with real coefficients has a complex root $a + bi$ 
(where $b \neq 0$), then its conjugate $a - bi$ is also a root.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Finding a polynomial from complex roots
content.problem: Find a quadratic equation with real coefficients having $3 - 2i$ as a root.
content.solution:
By the conjugate root theorem, $3 + 2i$ must also be a root.

Using Vieta's formulas:

- Sum of roots: $(3 - 2i) + (3 + 2i) = 6$

- Product of roots: $(3 - 2i)(3 + 2i) = 9 + 4 = 13$

The equation is: $x^2 - 6x + 13 = 0$

content.title: The Imaginary Numbers
content.content:
Italian mathematicians Tartaglia, Cardano, and Bombelli developed 
complex numbers in the 1500s while solving cubic equations. Rafael 
Bombelli (1526–1572) was the first to work systematically with complex 
arithmetic. The term "imaginary" was coined by René Descartes in 1637, 
and Leonhard Euler introduced the notation $i$ for $\sqrt{-1}$ in 1777.

content.text: The Complex Plane

Complex numbers can be visualized on the **complex plane** 
(also called the Argand plane):

items: The horizontal axis represents the real part
The vertical axis represents the imaginary part
The complex number $a + bi$ corresponds to the point $(a, b)$

content.title: Modulus

marginNotes.text:
Can you fathom the mysteries of God? Can you 
probe the limits of the Almighty?

marginNotes.author: Leibniz
marginNotes.text:
From the irrationals are born the impossible or 
imaginary quantities whose nature is very strange but whose usefulness 
is not to be despised.
